old_claim.note: Scientific Illustration Printing provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Scientific illustration is not a prerequisite for the scoped 1474 printed university textbooks, which predate the illustration node by decades.

source_shape.source_locator: Project summary: Leuven's first printed textbook appeared in 1474 with Aristotle and Porphyry texts for Faculty of Arts students; the corpus covers 410 teaching-linked editions.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: KU Leuven documents university textbooks as a teaching and print-production practice beginning in 1474 without making scientific illustration constitutive.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The directly dated textbook practice precedes the graph's 1543 scientific-illustration node, and many curricular text editions required no diagrams at all.

ontology_before: Printed university textbooks were placed downstream of a later, optional scientific-book illustration practice.
ontology_after: Printed university textbooks depend on printing and university curricula while scientific illustration remains an optional later specialization.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: printed_standardized_textbooks no longer directly depends on scientific_illustration_printing.
would_reject_if: The 1474 Leuven textbook were shown to require printed scientific illustrations or the illustration node were redated earlier than that textbook practice.
short_reason: The dated textbook corpus predates and does not require scientific illustration.
